Prepaid - Long Validity - Free/Cheap Data GUIDE Good for Kids, Parents, In-Laws, Backup I've been doing some research regarding the cheapest way to get extended validity and at least a slow speed data plan. All with minimal cost. It's an ongoing process so still a lot of ???. In my case it's for my kid who won't be making many calls nor accessing the internet except on wifi but would love to have Whatsapp active constantly. In other words, my criteria is (1) extended validity followed by (2a) slow data plan or a (2b) cheap fast data plan. I'd also like to avoid Data PAYU entirely. Here's what I have so far. Any additions, suggestions or corrections welcome. Who is this Guide for? People looking for: 1) Long validity and a free slow speed data plan for minimal expense. Reload and forget once a year. 2) Long validity and a cheap long validity data plan so you don't have to reload every month. 3) Long validity only with no data plan. Good for backup phones. Avoid Data PAYU for this or turn off mobile data. 4) Long validity data only plan. Good for tablets. Coming Soon. Notes: - Voice/SMS Rates are off-net. - Voice calls are listed for 1 min calls. Actual charging blocks varies. - Plans and rates change constantly so always double check. - Only plans with voice are listed. Might add data only plans later. Depends on what you're looking for. For me currently, I'm looking at either Buzzme or Digi. What I want is the cheapest upkeep cost, long validity and always-on Whatsapp (ie: any data even low speed is OK).

The statements below is based on the assumption that the intended user is a passive user (one who requires a line for receiving calls with minimum out calls/sms/data). XOX offers the same 28 months validity plus the option for 1 year extended validity @ RM25 (no further topup required). OneXOX does not have 1year extended validity option. The only way to extend after the 28 months is by reloading (and also user-get-user can work too but what if all your referrals are also passive user? ). Another way is to mnp out to another and back in the next day .....
It is impossible to reach the 28 months validity/ expired bcos the validity will be extended with every topup.

On the contrary, it is very much possible to have the 28 moths validity expires by way of : 1. Parking a number and forget about it for the next 28 months 2. Passive users who requires a number just for receving call and sms ( mnp to get rm5 preloaded credit and 28 months validity = cheap!) Doesn't oneXOX promotes maximum savings? The two above is already substantial savings for some .....
That's good to hear. Not so worried now.

Friends & Family: 1st 10 seconds FREE (On net only) On-Net (12 lines) 10 cents / Minute Off-Net (3 lines) 16 cents / Minute Port-In Charges: Top-up RM30 Free admin fee only, otherwise RM10 admin fee and RM9 for a new Tron SIM card. Every few months the promotion changes, but fundamentally they are the same. http://www.tron.com.my/tron-lite-sim/rates/ http://www.tron.com.my/tron-lite-sim/whats-new/ The plan may not be better than some mentioned here. But it offers flexibility. Daily, weekly, monthly plan on top of 365 validity, plus free calls and first 10seconds free if Friends and Family. The plan is superb if you need two of them, one needs monthly plan, the second totally just for receiving calls and making those 10 second calls to the first number. Pay RM100, get RM125 can use one year internet at RM10 monthly (200MB 3G speed +200MB @32kbps). Hope the information helps. As mentioned, there is no one plan better than all the rest in all aspects. Extra: Can talk time transfer with just 50sen total costs. Can totally disable PAYU by writing to TRON to fully disable Internet but then cannot subscribe to any plans and thus no internet in case of emergency. Ultimately decided to go DiGi. Went to do my MNP from Hotlink to Best Prepaid at a DiGi Store on Jan 31. The entire process was exceptionally fast. I was in and out in 10 mins. The actual process took less than 3 mins. Quite impressed with their efficiency. I just gave the nice woman my number and IC. Told them my requirements. Heard some keyboard sounds and before she could finish explaining to me that I'll need to reply to the MNP SMS, the SMS already arrived on the phone. Collected my new SIM card from her. Now it's just a matter of waiting for the process to complete. Total cost: RM0. Update: MNP completed sometime in the early evening of Jan 1. Just over 24 hrs. Reloaded RM50 Purchased Super Long Life for 1 year validity - RM38 IDD Internet Club for always-on Whatsapp - RM10 Music Freedom for streaming from Spotify - RM0 Still left 11.51 credit. I have been on Hotlink Tablet Plan for a few months now. Details as below : Hotlink Tablet Plan: Where to get : Only from Maxis Centers. No dealers carry them. SimPack Price: RM30 - Discount from RM 60. Data :1GB Data.   (No PAYU. No data access once quota is used up). Roaming : NIL. Speed : 4G/3G. Validity : 365 days upon SIM activation (For 1st year even if Data have been used up. There after, follows Top Up validity.). Top Up options once initial 1GB Data used up: RM25 - 1GB - Validity : 60 days. RM45 - 2GB - Validity : 365 days. Calls: NIL. SMS: NIL. MMS: NIL. Every Hotlink Tablet SIM comes with a mobile number but that is just to identify the SIM and account. Since there is no USSD commands via SMS, you will need to download the Hotlink RED app. Hotlink RED app functions: (*App can only function when SIM is installed in Tablet or Cellphone.) 1) Activate SIM for first use. 2) Check Balance of Data. 3) Validity. 4) Top Up. *Note: Wifi must be turn off and must be on Hotlink/Maxis network to use the Hotlink RED app. It is free to use and does not count towards your data. The only minor inconvenience of this plan is Top Up. 1) Top Up via Hotlink RED app : No issues. 2) Top Up at top up centres : There is no specific Top Up for Tablet plan so you have to top up RM 60 for the RM 45 2GB option. The balance will remain with the account as informed by Hotlink. 3) Top Up via Maxis Website : Same issue as no 2). Gadget to use with : 1) Tablet. 2) Dual SIM cellphones. 3) MiFi devices.   - * Cannot access Hotlink RED app with MiFi devices. This plan will work well if you have a Dual SIM phone or a MiFi device. It works as I am using it on my 3G MiFi device without issues. I use the Hotlink Tablet SIM as a back up when my cellphone hotspot is acting up. SPAM - SCAM Calls & Messages Awareness Guideliness The Malaysian Communications and Multimedia Commission (MCMC) has proposed several measures to clamp down on spam. MCMC issued a discussion papers in which it listed out several proposals to combat the incidence of unsolicitied e-mail and SMS (short message service). The proposals include formulating a definition of spam for regulatory purposes, imposing mandatory measures on service providers, and a multi-tier plan to resolve spam-related complaints and disputes. If the problem recurs or becomes excessive, MCMC suggested that users lodge complaints with service providers, failing which a complaint could be lodged with the Consumer Forum, or with the Content Forum for issues related to the content of the unsolicited messages. As a final recourse, users could complain to MCMC. To combat spam originating from overseas, MCMC proposed efforts to initiate the setting up of regional and “possibly” global collaborative alliances with foreign regulators, service providers, non-government organisations, and related projects and initiatives in other parts of the world. Promoting services via SMS is gaining popularity because of the mobile penetration rate in Malaysia has surpassed 100%. However because of the non-existence of the specific guideline regarding the SMS promotions, the SMS for advertising has become free for all industry. As an association we would like to develop and nurture this new advertising channel so it would not be misused and cast a negative impression from the public. Enclosed is a proposed guideline on the SMS Promotion that should be adhered to by all the CPs. The common and standardised guidelines between TELCO and MCMC to the consumers are: English Version 1. Reply STOP and send to (Received Gateway) eg. STOP send to 32777 2. Reply STOP ALL and send to (Received Gateway) eg. STOP ALL send to 32777 Bahasa Melayu Version 1. Reply BATAL and send to (Received Gateway) eg. BATAL send to 32777 2. Reply BATAL SEMUA and send to (Received Gateway) eg. BATAL SEMUA send to 32777 Additional Information Telco should activate a catch-all SMS that being sent to 6 series. If a recipient reply to the 6 series broadcast, with the word STOP, then all the future SMS promo for all the broadcast should be stopped. ON PROMO - Activate all promotions (chargeable) OFF PROMO - Stop all promotions (non-chargeable) Above commands are also applicable for promotion types of SMS messages. With this method, only one database should be maintained for each telco. All the SMS promo via 6 series will consult this database first before broadcast. Enabling reply to 6 series will make it easier for the recipient to Opt-Out without the need to send to a 3 series shortcode. Note: Above commands are agreed by the commision as FREE OF CHARGE and telecommuncations operator has no authority to charge the consumer for above listed commands. Additional Readings SMS Promotion prohibited to be sent are as follows: » Click to show Spoiler - click again to hide... « Suggestive Content A genre of texting, containing either text, images or video that is intended to be sexually arousing and could be interpreted to enticement, and the enjoyment of active attraction, or allurement of the offering content. E.g: "Dapatkan video terhangat pelajar universiti sekarang!" ('the word terhangat' or 'hottest' clearly relates to 'sexiness' rather than a literal meaning itself in this message) False Advertising A genre of texting, describing products or services which turned out not to be the way as it offered. E.g.: "Muat turun 3GP yang lucu sekarang!" (but it turned out to be a video in other format, not 3GP as stated in promo message) Misleading SMS Utilising words such as descriptive terms to increase the perceived value of a product or services offered. E.g: "Langgan sekarang untuk muat turun video dan lagu popular secara percuma" (the word percuma is used to attract subscriber but charges will still be imposed on the subscribers after some period and it doesnt stated clearly in the promo message). Number 6xxxx I'm not sure, but almost all the SMS received from 6xxxx are promoting or advertising from third parties. So, if you received an SMS from 6xxxx or 3xxxx number, please be careful and read it carefully. Style and sentence structure used is messy and sometimes 'fragmented'. There is a mixture of Malay and English. Normally the words most often used by service providers is as 'Register free', 'Without the charge list', 'free SMS mobile content', and others. Maybe then you are not sure or no knowledge to distinguish between genuine and fake SMS, and you have to register / subscribe. Serves this fool and take credit / phone bill. How to get out of this crisis? Follow the steps below, FIRST: Send command to cancel service the same number you used to register. For example, if you send 'ON section' to the number 30000, and to cancel service, send 'OFF section' or 'STOP section' to the same number (30 000). Your subscription will be canceled upon receipt of the SMS confirmation from the relevant authorities. Conversely, if the command 'OFF' or 'STOP' will not cause any effect (no response) or you forget the name of the service you subscribe to, try to 'STOP ALL' and wait for a response from the parties concerned. In most cases, the command 'STOP ALL' this is effective. If this also does not have any effect, then the next step is necessary. SECOND: Make a call to your network and tell them the problems you face. Your network can help identify to services you subscribe to accidental and provides guidance on how to cancel it. Calls must be made to the Customer Service operator, respectively. Conclusion Many think that network operators have the ability to prevent (block) numbers of external service providers. Ability to block is there, but the operator does not have the power to prevent a number unless directed in writing or direct instructions from the authorities (police, government and others). In addition, there are other users who may use the service and prevent the action would cause the number of users can not use the service. In conclusion, consumers should investigate and carefully read any SMS received from a third party. If you are unsure of the contents of the SMS or its contents very suspicious, ignore, and if able to save SMS as evidence if there is any problem in the future. The Executive Office of the Communications and Multimedia Content Forum of Malaysia (CMCF) hereby refers to your email dated xxxx with regard to the SMS spams which you have received. For your kind information, the CMCF is an independent self-regulatory body set up via the Communications and Multimedia Act 1998 (CMA’98) and operates under the auspices of the Malaysian Communications and Multimedia Commission (SKMM) to monitor and promote industry self-regulation of content over the electronic networked medium. The CMCF governs content by self-regulation in line with the Malaysian Communications and Multimedia Content Code (Content Code) which is a set of industry guidelines on the usage and/or dissemination of content for public consumption. SPAM is the common term for electronic ‘junk mail’ – messages sent to a person’s mobile phone that they have not consented to receive; in order to advertise a business or service, or invite you to sign up a subscription-based service. The content of spam messages varies. Some messages promote products or services, while others attempt to trick users into providing bank account or credit card details. Many spam messages contain offensive or fraudulent material. If you have received any spam messages on your mobile through any short codes, below are the attempts that you may take in order to stop it. Unsubscribed Text Messages Reply to the text message by typing STOP, OUT, END, QUIT or CANCEL to the sender. Subscribed Text Messages If you wish to unsubscribe to a text messaging service which you have subscribed to before, you may reply to the message by typing UNSUBSCRIBE Additional to that, we would like to suggest for you to contact your mobile service provider to seek for their assistance on blocking the sender from being able to send you any SMS in the future. For further information on SMS Spams and how to prevent/stop it, kindly visit the websites below: · http://www.skmm.gov.my/FAQs/Mobile-Content...%28SPAM%29.aspx · http://www.cfm.org.my/files/pdf/cfm-update...ates-100330.pdf As such, the Complaints Bureau treats this matter as closed and extends its sincere thanks to you for submitting the complaint. not sure what you guys heard bout MNP. But basically here's the whole concept. MNP allows you to port your number currently the number is tied to( exm. 012 - Maxis, 019 - Celcom and 016 - Digi etc), to any other telco service provider that you choose. So meaning if you are using 0128888000 which is now currently tied to Maxis, you want to change to Digi. In current practice, you cancel your Maxis subscription and register Digi, some even fanatic to get back the same 7 digit, 8888000 of course with 016 prefix instead. MNP may be launched nationwide in October PETALING JAYA: Mobile phone users in the Klang Valley may be able to get a taste of mobile number portability (MNP) by September, but the rest of the country will have to wait till end-October. The original deadline for the implementation of the MNP is mid-August but Malaysian Communications & Multimedia Commission (MCMC) wants to wait to make sure all systems are integrated to avoid any technical glitches when MNP is implemented. “The MCMC wants to ensure that it can monitor and manage a small area before implementing MNP nationwide. It will be easier to manage a smaller area although the concentration of mobile users is the highest in the Klang Valley,” said an industry source. MNP allows mobile users to switch networks while retaining their numbers. The Government first got public and industry feedback on the need to implement MNP in 2004. MNP was initially scheduled to be implemented in 2006. There is no denying that certain players were not ready until recently for fear of losing subscribers in a MNP environment. They also took a while to cooperate because the implementation was complex, said the source. Yet the issues, from porting charges (said to be between RM20 and RM25) to the validation time (anything from six hours to one week), need to be ironed out. In addition, the mobile user needs to be educated on the advantages of MNP so that MNP is a success in this country. The greatest fear of operators is the loss of subscribers and analysts have repeatedly said DiGi.Com Bhd will be the sure winner in an MNP environment. Only time will determine that. MNP may be good and it has been recognised by regulators as a key facilitator for competition. But the question is, with all the monies spent to implement MNP, who would get the lion’s share? Is it the consumer, the operators or the clearing house, Talian Gerak Alih Sdn Bhd, which is a third-party operator? Singapore was the first country to implement number portability in 1997 but it was a huge failure as it adopted a two-number system. MNP was re-launched in Singapore recently. In comparison, it has been a huge success in Hong Kong in 1999 and Australia in 2001. It was reported that in the US about 7.8 million mobile subscribers ported their numbers in the first 12 months of MNP’s introduction there. In Hong Kong 60% of subscribers changed operator and that led to an average price reduction of 60%. The reason for MNP’s success in Australia was because it is a “quick, seamless and low-cost experience.” URL: http://biz.thestar.com.my/news/story.asp?f...80&sec=business

Want a fast, extensive plan for mobile in Malaysia? Get our Season Pass: enjoy free calls, free SMS, or free data and share it with your family and friends! 1. Who can subcribe to Season Pass? Any existing subscriber of XOX Prepaid Plus, High Speed Pack, Penang #1758 and UDP will be able to enjoy Season Pass. (including OLD XinXun 3-tiers) 2. How can I subscribe to Season Pass? There are two ways you can subscribe to Season Pass: You can make your subscription purchase via SMS which will be deducted from your current available airtime. You can logon to http://onlinestore.xox.com.my/and make your purchase via Credit Card or Online Banking. 3. How many friends or family can I share my MINUTES, DATA and SMS credits with? Unlimited as long as you, your friends and family members are currently XOX Prepaid subscribers, you will be able to share without any limits or restrictions. RM0.50 will be charge to SENDER for each successful sharing. Multiple items sharing is ALLOWED in single share command SMS and will be only charge RM0.50 for successful sharing. Eg: Share 0103333010 D1 V50 S100 1234. (to share 1GB | 50 Mins | 100 SMS) 4. What is the maximum sharing? Voice - Unlimited SMS - Unlimited Data - 10GB 5. What is the validity period? The validity period follows your current XOX Prepaid Plus subscription. *For new Prepaid Plus user, you’ll need to subscribe Data M in order to utilize the data you purchase from Season Pass Package. 6. Once I have purchased MINUTES or SMS when will the consumption period begin? The consumption period will begin immediately upon successful purchase and subscription of Season Pass and right upon your next voice call or outgoing text message (LOCAL ONLY). NOTE: IDD will be as normal pay-per-use consumption via normal airtime deduction. 7. Once I have purchased Data when will the consumption period begin? The consumption period will begin upon your next your next data usage/consumption eg when you purchase Data Package SP D1 (5GB) it will be stored in your XOX account, it will only be deducted once you have commenced with NEW data activation eg Data D3, Data M XD18, Data M XD38 and Data M XD68. NOTE: Data Promo 1.5GB @ RM29.99 is NOT allowed to consume from Season Pass. Once you activate Data Promo, RM29.99 will deduct from your airtime as per normal behaviour.

5. Mengapa perlu ada Akaun Dedikasi (DA) dan Akaun Utama (MA)? Dengan pelan fleksi 2-dalam-1 yang unik, ONEXOX menawarkan caj kadar murah yang lebih penjimatan pada baki DA dan caj kadar biasa yang berpatutan pada baki MA. Mekanisme caj bermula dengan penolakan pada baki DA (kadar murah) terlebih dahulu; seterusnya menolak baki MA (kadar biasa) apabila kehabisan baki DA. Baki MA juga digunakan untuk melanggan pelan internet mudah alih serta membuat pindahan kredit ke pihak lain; sila pastikan anda mempunyai baki yang mencukupi sebelum meneruskan transaksi tersebut. Ketelusan ONEXOX mengadakan perbezaan kadar pada kedua-dua akaun tersebut adalah bertujuan untuk menawarkan kadar tetap sepanjang masa tanpa perlu membuat apa-apa promosi kadar yang berbeza-beza di masa hadapan. Berkonsepkan belian top up secara pukal, pelanggan-pelanggan ONEXOX yang membuat top up pada denominasi yang besar sekali gus akan menikmati lebih penjimatan pada baki DA. Kami mengalu-alukan agar semua pelanggan ONEXOX menggunakan ruang penjimatan ini bagi mengoptimakan perbelanjaan telekomunikasi harian anda. Rancanglah pembelian top up anda selepas ini! Define too expensive pls Compare with?

OneXOX rates are multilevel. Depends on DA/MA balances ... I can imagine the scenario ... Someone just mnp to onexox, it comes with RM5 credit in MA and starts making a call to an off-net number for 3 minutes and 31 seconds. The MA rate for off-net calls is 12sen/30seconds block. This translates to 8 (30 secs) blocks x 12sen totalling 96 sen. In comparison, the same 3 mins 31 secs call to off-net with DA balance would cost (8 blocks x 9 sen) 72 sen. While an on-net call (with DA balance) for 3 mins 31 secs would be ((4 x 7) + (4 x 8)) = 60 sen. Looking at FnF (limited to 10 numbers) rate, a 3 mins 31 secs call would cost (8 blocks x 6 sen) 48 sen. To be fair, oneXOX MA rate is equivalent to existing prepaid plans from Big 3 telcos of 12sen/30sec block. The Big 3 telcos appears to have standardized the rates at 24sen/minute calls and 12sen per sms. The cheapest prepaid rate to all networks is still Umobile power prepaid at 6sen/30secs block (but coverage may be an issue to some). Second cheapest would be SpeakOUT at 8sen/30secs block. Third would be Umobile older "new" prepaid plan at 10sen/30secs block. All plans mentioned is still available now. Has anyone noticed that Umobile has quietly revised their rates from 5sen/30secs to 6sen/30secs now? ..... Let's see how long can they maintain that position before revising upwards again ..... I am more positively inclined to onexox ever since they start offering extended validity and season pass. It is a good move and I believe that the next progression would be to just consolidate the DA/MA rates into one very competitive calling/sms rates. Forget about 5sen for 1st 10 secs ..... I already tried making under 10 secs calls which fails miserably most of the time.

On average... 1/3 of my calls every months falls within the 10secs block... It depends on the person, I think... And btw... hangs up around 7-8secs when making calls as the counter (on the server) are still ticking for ~1-3 secs before the calls are completely disconnected... After you've bought a Season Pass package, it will be valid as long as your OneXOX line. In order to draw data for usage during this period, you just have to subscribe to a Mobile Internet pass. Buy season pass 8GB = RM109.90 (1GB +- RM13.70) In order to use the season pass quota, need to buy any standard data plan (1.5GB is promo plan) Eg: want to use 1GB from season pass, just buy 1GB standard  data plan Then 1GB will be deducted from season pass quota, no credit will be deducted If not buy any standard data plan, the season pass will remain following credit validity which can last until 28 months Fyi 1GB standard data plan is RM38 Got season pass happy hour every thursday This post has been edited by adiwarna : Sep 18 2016, 05:56 PM

Like I said already, it's too complicated. That's the only reasonable conclusion when you see how many questions have to be asked and we are still half clueless. Having to buy a standard package, without paying for it, in order to use something else we did pay for, is just bizarre.

The Season Pass is actually quite easy to understand. Just think about making a big deposit (buy a Season Pass package) into your bank account (your OneXOX prepaid account). When you want to make withdrawals of smaller amounts, just use the same method as anyone who wants to use data - subscribe to the appropriate Mobile Internet/Data pass. The only difference is that you won't have to pay (again) for the (data) withdrawal. I THINK OneXOX is just using an existing platform (as in the Mobile Internet/Data passes here) to work with the Season Pass feature. Pretty straightforward, I feel, otherwise can always ask adiwarna here if you're unsure about anything. If you don't keep an open mind to understand something new, it'll just remain complicated if you choose to feel this way. Up to this point, I feel OneXOX listens to what users hope for. When I first got my SIM card, it's only for the 28-month validity. I thought then that their data packages were more expensive, but they started giving extras after. I was also hoping for Season Pass after looking through the XOX website, and it happened. Then we were wondering what's going to happen after the 28-month validity, and then they started having 6-month and 1-year validity extension. Which other prepaid card makes your data available for as long as your line? Not forgetting also that any subsequent top up you make will extend the validity even further, instead of just overlapping like the others? Or choose whichever prepaid makes you comfortable. The possible cause of CD became slower due to merger: loss of band 7 (2600) Band 7 (2600) contribute to the speed of LTE pre merger. After merger, CD have to surrender their 2600 band licences, therefore new integrated network losses out on band 7 for speed. Now CD has to rely on band1,  3 and 8 (2100, 1800, 900) for their post merger LTE network
